intro: 2007. You run Research In Motion. The BlackBerry is the phone of presidents and CEOs — its keyboard and secure email are an addiction people call 'CrackBerry.' Apple just announced a keyboard-less touchscreen phone. Business, you're sure, will never trust a toy.

realOutcome.valuation: phone business effectively worth zero by 2016
realOutcome.summary: BlackBerry went from defining the smartphone to abandoning phones entirely. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: The iPhone launches. It has no physical keyboard, weak security by enterprise standards, and terrible battery life. Your leadership calls it a consumer toy that serious professionals will reject.

choices.text: Dismiss it. Business needs a keyboard and security.
choices.reveal: RIM's co-CEOs genuinely believed the touchscreen was a fad and that physical keyboards and battery life would keep BlackBerry dominant. They were describing a museum exhibit.

choices.text: Crash-build a true touchscreen BlackBerry now.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You take the iPhone seriously in 2007 and pair a modern touchscreen with the security and keyboard-optional hardware enterprises trust. BlackBerry rides the smartphone wave up, not down.

choices.text: Open BBM to every platform while it's still loved.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: BBM was the messaging app everyone wanted. Freed from BlackBerry hardware in 2007, it could have been WhatsApp before WhatsApp existed.

decisions.scenario: The pressure to answer the iPhone is intense. You can rush a touchscreen phone — the Storm — to market this year, before it's really ready, or take the time to build it right.

choices.text: Rush the Storm out to answer the iPhone fast.
choices.reveal: The BlackBerry Storm shipped in 2008 with a bizarre clicking screen and buggy software. Carriers reportedly returned huge numbers of units, and the rushed answer damaged the brand more than silence would have.

choices.text: Take the time to build the touchscreen right.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A polished first touchscreen — even a year later — preserves the brand's reputation for quality instead of torching it on a half-finished gimmick.

choices.text: Skip touch; double down on keyboards plus great apps.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You own the keyboard niche unapologetically and pour resources into a real app ecosystem. A smaller but defensible BlackBerry survives with loyalists.

decisions.scenario: Developers are pouring into Apple's App Store and Android. Your platform is clunky to build for. You can invest heavily in apps and developer tools, or protect your crown jewels — email and BBM.

choices.text: Protect email and BBM; neglect the app ecosystem.
choices.reveal: RIM under-invested in apps, betting its email and messaging edge would hold. But a smartphone without apps became unsellable, and the ecosystem gap turned into a canyon.

choices.text: Invest heavily in apps and world-class dev tools.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You give developers a reason to build for BlackBerry. Apps plus your security and keyboard keep enterprise and prosumers on your platform.

choices.text: Adopt Android to inherit its entire app library.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You put the world's apps on hardware people already trust for security and typing. BlackBerry becomes the premium enterprise Android phone.

decisions.scenario: You're launching the PlayBook tablet to counter the iPad. There's a problem: at launch it won't have a native email app — on a device from the company that defined mobile email.

choices.text: Ship the PlayBook now, without native email.
choices.reveal: The PlayBook launched in 2011 with no native email client, a humiliating gap for the email company. It flopped, and RIM took a massive writedown on unsold inventory.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You hold the launch until the tablet does the one thing your brand is famous for. A complete product protects the reputation a broken one destroys.

choices.text: Cancel the tablet; focus everything on phones.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You refuse to fight Apple on tablets and pour the money into fixing your phone platform, where you still have a fighting chance.

decisions.scenario: It's late. You can bet everything on a brand-new operating system, BlackBerry 10, arriving years after iOS and Android locked up the market — or pivot the company toward what it might actually survive as.

choices.reveal: BlackBerry 10 launched in 2013 to a market that had already moved on. Sales were dismal, RIM took a $4.4B loss, and it exited the consumer phone business soon after.

choices.text: Pivot early to secure software and services.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You accept the phone is lost and become the security and enterprise software company BlackBerry eventually — belatedly — became to survive at all. Doing it in 2013 saves years and billions.

choices.text: Sell the company at its 2008 peak instead.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You read the writing on the wall while you're still worth $80B and hand shareholders an enormous exit at the top. Timing the sale beats fighting the tide.

bands.line: You saw the touchscreen coming and adapted. Villain episode: WON.
A smaller, focused survivor. Not the king, but not a ghost.
The keyboard company that the touchscreen erased. Faithful to history. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Apple erased BlackBerry's lead in five years. Today's agents flagged a market leader calling its disruptor a toy →
